sections.assessment: The chapter has a style — disciplined, controlled, liturgically rhythmic — but style is not the same as voice, and this is exactly where King's central criterion exposes the problem. A King narrator is present in the room with you; this narrator is invisible in the wrong way, having evacuated the prose of editorializing, personality, or any sense of a human being who is telling you this story rather than recording it. What you get instead is a register — cool, formal, almost hagiographic — that impersonates voice through repetition and rhythm. The technique is consistent to a fault. But consistency is not personality. King's principle is that the reader should feel a real person talking to them, and this narrator has no opinions, makes no asides, offers no angle on events. The prose is also visibly stylized in a way King warns against: 'which was the way of a man who' appears as a mechanical tic, the triple-beat parallelism of cart descriptions circles back on itself, the relentless trailing relative clauses flatten every paragraph into the same cadence. These draw attention to the writing rather than pulling attention through it toward the story. The chapter's emotional undertow — Augustine recognizing, without naming, what Stephen feels for Possidius — is genuine and affecting, and the cold disc carries real atmospheric weight. But these are achievements of subject matter, not of narrator voice. The storyteller never leans in, never signals 'watch this.' The most King-adjacent moments — small psychological observations that approach personality — are isolated human acts in an otherwise clinically administrative narration. The narrator has no position. Not skeptical, not admiring, not ironic. That is the problem.
good_examples: 'The address came out of him now in the way an old habit came back to a man whose mind was on a thing he could not entirely manage.' — King-adjacent: psychological, specific, earned. The narrator briefly becomes a person making an observation about human behavior rather than a recording apparatus.

'The disc was cold. / It had been cold the night the boy from the lower-town school had died, four years before.' — Short declaratives, atmospheric repetition that builds genuine unease. The tonal shift here — something cold and slightly wrong in the world — is the chapter's best vocal moment, closest to King's instinct for the uncanny in everyday objects.
bad_examples: 'The cart came to the basilica gate at the second hour of the afternoon, having travelled the lower coast road through the night and the morning under armed escort that the proconsul's office at Carthage had grudgingly provided after the senior bishop had pressed for three days.' — This is an archivist's sentence, not a storyteller's. Logistics delivery with no personality behind it. King would cut this to its bones and let the narrator comment on what matters: the cart, the man on it, what Augustine felt watching it come.

'He was managing in the way Stephen had managed across the years he had been in the household, which was the way of a man who had decided some long time ago that the management was the work and that the management did not require permission to feel less than it appeared to feel.' — The narrator analyzes from sociological remove. This is a biographer's observation, not a storyteller's. The emotional content should arrive through scene — through the cloth in Stephen's hand, the count of three before he looks away — not through authorial explanation. King's cardinal rule: the scene does the work. The narrator does not explain the scene.
suggestions: Give the narrator one moment of direct presence — not much, just one line where the storyteller's angle is visible. 'Augustine had received many carts up that road. He had not received his friend.' That break in register signals a human being is behind the prose.

Vary sentence length aggressively. The relentless long-sentence rhythm flattens the chapter into a single drone. One short sentence in the opening paragraph — 'The mule was tired.' placed earlier, standing alone — would reset the reader's ear and prove the narrator has control, not compulsion.

Cut 'which was the way of a man who' as a construction. It appears in three variations and is a tic the prose discipline rules already flag. Replace it with scene or cut it entirely — the behavior on the page already shows us the way of the man.

Trust the Stephen scene to carry its emotional payload without the narrator's sociological summary. Show us the count of three, the face turned to the wall, the count back. Stop there. The reader is smart enough to know what they're seeing. King's rule: trust the reader.

The narrator needs a position on Augustine — some angle that lets us know who is telling this story and why they care. Not a loud angle. A quiet one. The difference between 'He went to compline' and 'He went to compline, which was what he had, for thirty years, done when there was nothing else left to do' is the difference between recording and storytelling.

sections.assessment: This chapter is doing something formally accomplished and something deep-POV deficient at the same time, and the trouble is those two things are the same thing. The prose is beautiful — controlled, liturgical, repetitive in the way of a man telling beads — but it operates almost entirely at psychic distance three or four. We watch Augustine. We are told Augustine thinks. We observe him observing Stephen. We receive reports of his interior state. What we almost never get is Augustine's consciousness rendered from inside, in the moment, as it happens. King's principle is not complicated: cut the distance. Don't tell me he watched the cart come. Put me on the road with him, behind his eyes, feeling what a man feels when his best friend is on a pallet under canvas after someone tried to kill him. The chapter knows Augustine is afraid — the fever check, the wound surveillance, the half-hourly reports — but it never gives us the fear. It gives us the behavior that fear produces, narrated at a remove. The most serious structural problem is that Augustine's interior voice is indistinguishable from the prose narrator's voice. They share the same formal, subordinate-clause-heavy, inverted syntax. King's characters think in their own idioms. They have mental tics, associative leaps, the specific gravity of their particular obsessions. Augustine here thinks in the same register the omniscient narrator uses, which means we never feel we've gone deeper than the surface. The italicized dialogue functions as the chapter's best approximation of rendered interiority — spare, direct, immediate — and it works better than everything around it precisely because it drops the distancing apparatus. The bronze disc sequence is the chapter's genuine high point for interior monologue: specific, sensory, historically anchored in Augustine's grief, and moving toward a real psychological revelation. The Stephen-looking passage is the chapter's greatest missed opportunity: here is the emotional core, a recognizable human thing seen by a man who must decide what to do with recognition, and the chapter renders it in the most abstract possible language at the farthest possible remove.
good_examples: 'He had not, in those sixteen years, judged any of them worthy. He thought, sitting at the desk with the cold bronze in his hands and the lamp at his elbow and Possidius two cells away, that he had not yet found the man he would pass the box to and that the man, when he came, would come from a direction Augustine had not been watching.' — The sensory anchor (cold bronze, lamp, Possidius two cells away) earns the abstraction that follows. We are inside a specific skull at a specific moment.

'Stephen had not, on Augustine's hearing, used the address *father* to him in private speech in nine years. The address came out of him now in the way an old habit came back to a man whose mind was on a thing he could not entirely manage.' — This is observation shading into interpretation shading into emotional understanding, which is how deep POV actually works. We get Augustine reading Stephen, and in that reading we understand both men.
bad_examples: 'Augustine watched the cart come.' — A filtering verb doing nothing but keeping us outside. King would cut 'watched' and put us on the road: the cart's slow rocking, the mule's exhausted gait, the canvas top Augustine's eyes cannot stop returning to because under that canvas is the body of a man he loves and does not yet know is alive in any real sense. The distance here is a choice, but it is the wrong choice for the scene's emotional weight.

'He recognised that the looking had a meaning and that the meaning was a meaning he was not, on the present accounting of his attention, prepared to take in.' — This is the chapter's single most important interior moment — a man sees that his deacon is in love with his friend — and it is rendered in the most abstract possible language, wrapped in triple subordination, at maximum psychic distance. King would crack this open. What does Augustine's gut do when the recognition lands? Does something cold move through him? Does he look away? The recognition is the story and it is being described from outside itself.

The entire opening paragraph: 'The cart came to the basilica gate at the second hour of the afternoon, having travelled the lower coast road through the night and the morning under armed escort...' — Eleven lines of external narration establishing logistics. No one is behind any eyes. We don't know whose chapter this is. King opens in a character's consciousness and earns the world-building through that consciousness. Who is Augustine before we know what the cart's arrival means to him?

'The afternoon went the way an afternoon went when a bishop had to write four letters of consequence in the course of three hours.' — Summary narration at a glide. Four significant letters, each carrying enormous stakes, and we get none of the texture of writing them. The letter to the imperial vicar locked in the desk — what does it cost Augustine to lock it? King would spend a paragraph inside the act of sealing that letter.
suggestions: Eliminate filtering verbs as a discipline: search the manuscript for 'Augustine watched,' 'he looked,' 'he noticed,' 'he thought,' 'he saw,' 'he felt,' and cut them wherever possible, replacing with the direct perception. Instead of 'Augustine watched the cart come,' give us the cart — the mule's hooves on the harbor road, the way the canvas shifts, the sound the wheel makes at the dip in the road — and trust that we know it is Augustine receiving this because we are behind his eyes.

The chapter's emotional center is the cart arriving with Possidius on the pallet. We need thirty seconds inside Augustine's body before the cart reaches the gate — not thoughts about his episcopal history, but physical sensation: the weight in his chest, the quality of the light, something his hands are doing that he is not aware they are doing. King would give us the character's body under emotional stress before the plot arrives.

Differentiate Augustine's mental voice from the prose narrator's voice. Augustine is a man of fifty who has been writing theology for twenty years, who thinks in argument and counter-argument, who has a specific relationship to his own grief that he has theorized about extensively. Let that specificity crack through the surface. When he holds the cold disc, his mind should move the way Augustine's mind moves — not the narrator's.

The Stephen-looking passage needs to be rewritten from inside the moment of recognition, not after it. Give us Augustine watching Stephen watch Possidius sleep, give us the three-count of looking, give us the turn to the wall — and then give us the thing that moves through Augustine's chest when he understands what he is seeing. The abstraction ('a meaning he was not prepared to take in') can come after we have felt the recognition land. Right now it substitutes for the felt experience rather than following from it.

The four-letters paragraph is a missed opportunity to show Augustine's interior under pressure. Take one letter — the one to the imperial vicar he locks in the desk — and render the act of drafting and locking it from inside Augustine's consciousness. What is he afraid to send? What does the drawer closing feel like? That scene will do more for character than three paragraphs of summary.

sections.assessment: This chapter earns most of its characterization the right way — through behavior, ritual, and specific telling detail rather than through explanation. Augustine's character is established before a word of his backstory appears: he sets down the stylus, asks two questions, stands, walks to the door without speaking. Stephen stands with him and walks behind him. No interiority required. Stephen's defining trait — the folded hands at belt-level, the silence, the two-hour vigil at the gate, the walk down the road a quarter-mile and back — gives us a man before the prose ever tells us what kind of man he is. The bronze box is the chapter's most sophisticated backstory mechanism: fourteen years of grief (Monica at Ostia, Adeodatus at Tagaste, the dying schoolboy) delivered through physical ritual, a cold disc, and a single remembered sentence of dialogue. That's King's method exactly — the object as memory-carrier. Stephen's 'father' slip is a masterclass: nine years of relationship, a shift in emotional register, the whole weight of the moment compressed into one unrepeated word. And the chapter's best characterization is structural: Stephen doesn't come to the gate. His absence is the action that defines him, and Augustine's deliberate non-asking in response defines them both. Where the chapter falters is in its periodic impulse to explain what it has already shown — to analyze the mechanism after the mechanism has already done its work invisibly. King's prose is invisible. When it pauses to explain its own insight, the window shows.
good_examples: 'He had not, in the two hours at the gate, said a word. He had stood the way Stephen stood, which was with his hands folded in front of him at the level of his belt, and he had watched the road. He had been watching it longer than Augustine had been watching it because Stephen had, at the first hour, walked down the road for a quarter of a mile in the direction the cart would come from, and had walked back, and had taken his place at the gate, and had not moved since.' — Backstory and character delivered entirely through specific physical behavior. We know Stephen completely before he speaks.

'Augustine had not, on Augustine's hearing, used the address father to him in private speech in nine years. The address came out of him now in the way an old habit came back to a man whose mind was on a thing he could not entirely manage.' — Nine years of relationship history delivered through one unrepeated word. The entire emotional architecture of the bond is in this passage.

'Stephen looked at it the way a man looked at a face he was not, by the protocols of the household, permitted to look at the way he wished to look at it; and the looking went on for the count of three and then Stephen would turn his face to the wall or to his hands.' — Character revealed by what Stephen does with his eyes, not by what he thinks or feels. The reader names what Augustine won't.

'Stephen looked at him the way a man looked at a man who had been at the gate to see off a third man; the look was the look of a man who had, in not coming to the gate himself, made a decision he was prepared to be asked to defend and was not, on the evidence of his face, prepared to defend without difficulty.' — Character revealed through deliberate absence. This is the chapter's single best moment: everything we need to know about Stephen is in what he chose not to do.

'Carry it,' Monnica had said. 'The man it is for will know it. He will not be you.' — Backstory through remembered dialogue attached to physical action. Monica's voice delivers sixteen years of mission and loss in two sentences without a word of summary.
bad_examples: 'He thought: I have been watching for sixteen years and I have not, until this autumn, seen what was in front of me.' — This tells us the meaning of what we just felt. The bronze disc being cold, the recognition on the sixth day, Stephen at the door — we already have this. The italic thought turns earned ambiguity into a thesis statement.

'He had been, he understood as he set the box back, the kind of man who took the disc down on no particular occasion to check whether the man it was for had arrived in the household.' — The action of taking down the box, finding it cold, and replacing it already characterizes Augustine completely. This sentence is the caption under the painting. King would end on 'He went to compline' and trust the reader to have arrived at this understanding themselves.

'he had been the desk the household had organised itself around' — The first clause of this reflection ('the household had made itself around him') lands cleanly. The second elaboration ('he had been the desk the household had organised itself around') is a second metaphor for the same idea, and it slightly undermines the first by suggesting the writer doesn't trust it.

'the naming would, if performed, require him to do something with the named thing, and the something with the named thing would not, on any reading of the household and the friends, improve the household or the friends or the bishop two cells away' — This unpacks Augustine's psychology too fully. 'He did not name it. He carried it.' is the correct ending of this thought. Everything before that final line is the prose explaining itself.
suggestions: Trust the bronze box scenes to close without summary. When Augustine replaces the disc and goes to compline, the chapter is already complete. The November scene's final interpretive paragraph ('He had been, he understood...') should be cut entirely — the action has done the work.

The italic interiority at the chapter's close ('I have been watching for sixteen years') should be cut or converted into action. Let Augustine simply go to the cathedral. The watching-better thought is earned by the chapter but doesn't need to be stated; if it must appear, make it oblique — a physical gesture, not a formulated resolution.

The passage explaining why Augustine doesn't name what he's recognized ('the naming would, if performed, require him to do something...') runs three sentences past its landing point. 'He did not name it. He carried it.' is the whole thought. Cut the three preceding sentences.

Consider whether Augustine's thinking during the box scene ('He thought of Possidius two cells away... He thought of the wound on the shoulder...') needs the full inventory. King would select two items from the list and let white space carry the rest. The catalog form works here but could be trimmed by half without losing the accumulative weight.

Stephen's interiority is currently handled entirely through Augustine's observation — which is correct and strong. Protect this. Do not give Stephen a single italicized thought anywhere in the chapter. His silence and his eyes are more powerful than anything he could think at himself.

sections.assessment: This chapter has one genuine masterstroke — the cold bronze disc — and several smart tactile moments, but it largely renders its setting as geography rather than as inhabited, sensory experience. For a story set in Hippo Regius on the North African coast in August, the heat is almost entirely absent. A King reader would be sweating through the second hour of the afternoon. The harbor road, the basilica courtyard, the guest cell, the chapter house — these spaces are named and navigated but rarely felt. The chapter's restraint is deliberate and often serves the voice, but King's principle that setting does double duty (reveals character AND creates mood AND grounds the reader in a physical body) is only fully honored in the disc scenes. Where the chapter succeeds sensorially, it succeeds well: the cool hand that explains itself, the damp cloth that becomes a character study, the wine drunk for warmth rather than taste. The disc sequence is built on a single repeating tactile note — cold — that earns thematic weight through repetition and contrast. That is exactly how King uses a telling detail. But olfactory detail is almost entirely absent. A sick room in 403 CE, a mule on a harbor road, lamp oil burning at a writing desk through the night — none of these register as smell. Sound is minimal and mostly functional. The setting is intelligent but under-inhabited.
good_examples: 'The disc was cold.' / 'He held the disc for the count of perhaps a hundred... the warmth that had been in the bronze for the whole of an hour and had not, in any other moment in the sixteen years between then and now, been in the bronze at all.' — A single tactile adjective doing enormous structural and thematic work. Cold recurs like a refrain, and the one exception (Monnica's death, warmth for an hour) becomes the chapter's theological engine. This is King's telling-detail principle executed at its best.

'The hand was cool. The hand had been on a cart for thirteen hours and the cart had been jolted and the hand had been holding the rim of the pallet for the whole of those hours and the holding had taken what strength the hand had to give.' — The explanation of why the hand is cool is what elevates this from observed detail to felt truth. King always earns his sensory details this way — not just what, but why, and the why reveals the body's history.

'He was sitting with the cloth folded in his hand the way a man sat when the work he had been doing had been done for the present moment and the next moment of it had not yet come.' — A damp cloth at a bedside that tells us everything about Stephen's emotional state. The object is doing double duty: sensory anchor and character revelation.

'The hinges gave their small specific sound.' — 'Small specific' is doing quiet genius work here. We don't know the exact sound but we know it precisely — and we're told it carries the memory of two deaths. That's a sound that has weight before we hear it.
'He drank the wine for the warmth and not for the taste.' — Brief and perfect. Sensory and psychological at once. The wine as function rather than pleasure tells us where his mind is.
bad_examples: 'He went to the writing room. He sat at the desk... He went out. He went to the chapter house and stood in the chapter house alone.' — Augustine moves through the basilica repeatedly but we never inhabit those spaces. What does the writing room smell like — ink, parchment, lamp oil, stone? What does the chapter house feel like underfoot? We navigate the floor plan without ever arriving anywhere.

'The cart came up the harbour road in the slow methodical way a cart with a wounded man on the floor of it came up a road that had not been mended in three seasons.' — Strong procedural observation, but the harbour road goes unrendered. Hippo Regius is a port town. There should be a smell here — salt, fish, the particular heat that rises off stone roads in August in North Africa. The road's poor maintenance is noted but not felt.

'Possidius was on the pallet under the canvas. He was awake. His eyes were open and were tracking...' — The sick-room arrival is handled primarily in medical inventory (eyes tracking, bandaged head, swollen shoulder). A King scene would also register the smell of the wound, the particular close heat under a canvas roof, the way a man who has been on straw for six days and a cart for thirteen hours actually smells. The body is documented but not present.

'He ate the bread without paying attention to it.' — The missed opportunity here is not the inattention (which is psychologically accurate) but the absence of any prior sensory grounding for the bread. King would have given us the bread — its texture, its staleness, whatever makes it specific — so that Augustine's not-tasting it lands as loss rather than just notation.

The entire second and third weeks pass in summary with virtually no new sensory texture. 'The three of them worked through the late mornings of the second half of the third week in a routine...' — This is the writer summarizing instead of rendering. One well-chosen sensory detail — the smell of Stephen's ink, the quality of light through the writing-room window at noon — would anchor this whole passage and make the household feel real rather than reported.
suggestions: August in North Africa is a character King would never leave offstage. The heat pressing through stone walls at the second hour of the afternoon, the way the courtyard bakes and the cloister offers shadow — this costs you three sentences and pays off the entire setting. Put the heat in the chapter's opening movement and let it work through everything that follows.

The sick room needs at least one olfactory detail. You don't need to be graphic — King often uses a single restrained note (the particular smell of old linen, or the absence of smell where you'd expect it) rather than clinical description. 'The room had the smell of a man who had been on the road' or its equivalent. Right now the guest cell is a list of objects (cot, stool, basin, cloth) in a sensory vacuum.

The harbor quarter is mentioned twice (the physician comes from there) but never arrived in. Even a single ambient sound — a ship being loaded, voices at the harbor gate in the evening — would give Hippo Regius the quality of a real port city rather than a backdrop. King's settings are always larger than the scene they contain.

Use the chapter house scene more fully. Augustine stands at a small high window looking at the courtyard for the count of three hundred. This is a moment that asks for sensory grounding: the quality of the light (afternoon, late August), the sound from the courtyard, what the stone floor feels like under his feet after walking from the gate. Right now it's the most interior moment in the chapter and the most sensorially empty.

The cold/warm binary of the disc is your best device — protect it by giving the warmth a texture beyond the one reference to 'warmth in the bronze.' When Augustine remembers the hour of warmth at Ostia, let him remember it physically. What did it feel like in his hand? How did he know it was warm and not just body-warmed metal? The memory needs a body.

sections.assessment: This chapter builds dread almost entirely through accumulation and withholding — both legitimate King techniques — and at its best achieves something genuinely unsettling: a low-frequency hum of unease beneath absolute surface calm. The bronze disc is the chapter's engine. Cold on every checking, warm only once in sixteen years, tied to deaths and dying boys and drowned children, it operates exactly the way King's best objects operate: as a thermometer for something terrible we cannot name. The cart's slow arrival in the opening pages is paced masterfully — proconsular grudging, unmended roads, a freedman steadying the pallet at every dip — the ordinary logistics of a fifth-century diocese weaponized as delay, the reader held back from what they can already feel coming. The junior officer's hand on his sword hilt is the chapter's best piece of ambient threat: a single gesture that gives the Donatist violence a body. Stephen's forbidden looking at Possidius's sleeping face is the chapter's most unsettling human moment — King knows that dread lives in the ordinary, in what a man does when he believes no one is watching. Augustine witnessing it, naming the recognition internally, and choosing not to name it aloud creates a second species of dread: the thing that cannot be addressed is always more frightening than the thing that can. Where this chapter falls measurably short of King's standard is in its complete excision of the violence that caused everything. The attack on Possidius never has a body. Its wounds are administrative. King would give us one visceral flash — not the whole scene, just a single image that makes the reader feel what those bandages cover — because dread requires a real monster in the basement, not a monster described in the summary of a report. The ending closes down rather than opens out. King's final beats almost always leave one small hook of unease, one wrong note that makes the reader take the thought to bed. 'The disc was cold. He went to compline.' is beautiful, but it resolves. The dread should still be standing when the last word lands.
good_examples: 'alive but not entirely alive, with a wound to the head and a wound to the right shoulder and a number of further wounds the report had not particularised because the report had been brief' — the bureaucratic understatement of 'further wounds the report had not particularised' is the chapter's single most unsettling phrase. The omission does more work than any description could.

'the junior officer rode ahead of the mule with one hand on the bridle of his own horse and the other on the hilt of his sword, which was not unsheathed but was being kept ready, in the manner of a man who had been told to escort a bishop on the African coast in the year of the council of Carthage and was not yet certain the road was over' — ambient threat rendered perfectly. The road is not over. That's the whole chapter in one clause.

'Stephen looked at it the way a man looked at a face he was not, by the protocols of the household, permitted to look at the way he wished to look at it; and the looking went on for the count of three and then Stephen would turn his face to the wall' — the forbidden looking is King's kind of dread: domestic, specific, and completely without resolution.

'He had been judging men, in the back of his attention, for sixteen years. He had not, in those sixteen years, judged any of them worthy. He thought, sitting at the desk with the cold bronze in his hands...that the man, when he came, would come from a direction Augustine had not been watching.' — this is effective dramatic irony. The reader senses Augustine is already wrong about the direction.

'Augustine had not, in seven years as bishop, watched a cart come up that road in this way' — small, precise, does the work of a full paragraph of exposition. The ordinary road made strange by one specific qualifier.
bad_examples: 'In the autumn the Donatist proceedings in the next valley to Calama concluded with the conviction of two priests and the quiet exile of a third. The attack on the bishop on the road in August was the principal evidence.' — the attack, which is the chapter's originating violence, appears here as pure legal summary. King does not allow the monster to stay in the report. The reader needs one image from that road — a sound, a face, a single detail Possidius cannot stop seeing — or the wounds remain abstract.

'He had been, he understood as he set the box back, the kind of man who took the disc down on no particular occasion to check whether the man it was for had arrived in the household. The man had not arrived. The disc was cold. The bronze was bronze. He went to compline.' — this is a closing-down ending. The dread is folded away with the disc. King would end with the disc cold AND one wrong note: a sound in the corridor, Eraclius's face at the door with an expression that doesn't fit, something small and incorrect that the reader carries into the dark. Right now the chapter resolves when it should unsettle.

'The face under the bandage was not the colour of a man at the beginning of a fever, which was what Augustine had been intending, in the back of his mind, to check for at the seventh-hour visit.' — this is the physician's report disguised as interiority. By telling us what the fever-check reveals (no fever), the tension dissolves rather than tightens. King withholds the check's result, or has the check reveal something unexpected. Here the reader is reassured precisely when they should be held in suspense.

'The four letters took the afternoon. Eraclius brought word at the half-hour through the four hours. The reports were the reports a junior brought to the bishop in such cases: the wounds had been re-bandaged; the bishop had taken a small quantity of broth...' — the administrative summary of Possidius's recovery, however period-authentic, is a pacing dead zone. Four paragraphs of bureaucratic competence. King would cut this to two sentences and use the space for one moment of wrongness — a half-hour when Eraclius doesn't come, or comes with his face wrong.
suggestions: Give the attack a single physical image through Possidius's speech or Augustine's observation — something concrete that escaped the report. Not a scene, just one detail: the sound the road made, the specific way the first stone landed, what Possidius's last coherent thought was before the pallet. The wound on the head needs to have been made by something with a face.

End with the disc cold AND one unresolved wrong note. Augustine goes to compline — but on the way, he passes the guest cell, the door is open, the room is empty and made up for the next guest, and there is nothing wrong with any of it. Except the stool by the wall where Stephen sat for three weeks has been moved. It is now at the foot of the cot. No one is on it. Let that sit. Don't explain it. King trusts the reader to be afraid.

Activate the disc's rules earlier and more explicitly. Right now the reader learns the disc was warm once (Monnica's death) halfway through the chapter. Move that information forward, or repeat it at each checking in a single sentence, so that every coldness is weighted against the one warmth. The contrast is where the dread lives: warm means the man has arrived, and the man has never arrived, and Augustine is forty-nine and watching his household and the disc stays cold.

The dramatic irony in 'the man, when he came, would come from a direction Augustine had not been watching' is the chapter's best foreshadowing — but it needs a callback. At the departure scene, when Augustine is at the gate and Stephen is not, there should be one beat where Augustine consciously looks in the wrong direction before going back through the gate. Let him almost see what's in front of him. Then cut to the writing room. The reader will feel the near-miss.

Stephen's 'father' — used privately for the first time in nine years — is a superb crack in the armor, but it needs a physical correlative. After Augustine says 'The face will go back to being the face it was' and Stephen says 'I know, father. I am all right,' give us one thing Stephen does with his hands. Not much. King's characters always do something small with their hands when they're not all right. The cloth, the basin, the stool — pick one and give it one wrong gesture that the word 'father' won't cover.

sections.assessment: This chapter operates in an unusual register — sparse, italicized, stripped of quotation marks and most tags — and at its best this choice does exactly what King's best dialogue does: it forces the weight onto what's *not* said. The subtext work is exceptional. Possidius saying 'Stephen.' at the gate — one word, the only time he says the name in Augustine's hearing across four weeks — is a King-grade moment: everything acknowledged, nothing named, the reader doing the heavy lift. Stephen's involuntary 'father' after nine years of not using the address is equally strong; the narration earns it and the narrative doesn't over-explain it. Augustine choosing, at the final scene, not to say the thing he walked from the gate intending to say is the chapter's most sophisticated dialogue move — what goes *unspoken* is the scene's engine. However, the technique has failure modes here. When Augustine addresses the junior officer at the cart, the speech reads like a dictated memo, perfectly parallel and organized, lacking the small irregularities of actual command. The council-schedule exchange between Augustine and Stephen near the end slides into 'as you know, Bob' exposition — information the author needs the reader to have, delivered in implausibly structured sentences no real person assembles on the fly. And while the absence of contractions may be intentional period-register discipline, it smooths every voice to the same formal surface, making it difficult to distinguish Possidius's spoken cadence from Augustine's from Stephen's. The three characters sound like one author wearing three hats.
good_examples: '*Possidius.* / *Aurelius.*' — Two names as the whole of a greeting between a man arriving near-dead and the man receiving him. No stage direction needed. King would approve: the scene earns this economy over two pages of approach.

'*I am at Hippo. I am, I think, going to be at Hippo for some weeks.*' — The 'I think' is the only hedge in Possidius's speech across the chapter. A man uncertain of the extent of his own damage. Real. Unwritten.

'*Yes, father.*' / 'Augustine had not, on his hearing, used the address *father* to him in private speech in nine years.' — The dialogue does not signal this; the narration catches it afterward. The sequence is correct. This is how King plants the knife: let the line land, *then* explain the blade.

'*Stephen.*' — Possidius's one-word naming at the gate, 'the only time he had said the name, in Augustine's hearing, since he had been at Hippo.' Perfect. The author has been listening for it, the reader has been listening for it, and Augustine has been listening for it. One word does the work of a scene.

Augustine choosing NOT to say the prepared thing and substituting council logistics instead — the decision is dramatized through narration but the silence in the dialogue is the text. 'He had been intending...to say a thing that...he had decided not to say.' This is subtext at the structural level, not just the line level.
bad_examples: '*Captain. The bishop will be carried into the residence on the pallet. Your men may help. Stephen will direct. After the bishop is in the residence I will see you in the lay parlour. There will be wine. I will write the letter to the proconsul this afternoon for you to carry back at first light.*' — This is a written memo delivered as speech. Real command, even from a precise man, has texture: a pause, a revision, a repetition. This reads like Augustine handed the junior officer a bulleted list.

'*We will set the morning of the second day for the question of episcopal jurisdiction. The morning of the third day for the matter of the Donatist proceedings. The afternoon of the third day for the financial reconciliation between the dioceses. Possidius will preside at the first two; the senior bishop at the third. I will speak at the close of each day in my place as host.*' — Classic 'as you know, Bob.' The author needs the reader to understand the conference structure; Augustine delivers it in three perfectly parallel sentences no speaking human constructs spontaneously. The information could be parcelled across the narration, or compressed to a line and summarized.

Stephen's voice across the chapter is almost entirely 'Yes' and 'Yes, father.' This is a defensible choice — Stephen's reticence is characterization — but the one moment he speaks unprompted ('*You should eat*' / '*I will eat with you in the small parlour if you wish*') sounds identical in register and rhythm to Augustine. Two men in long proximity often do sound alike, but the reader needs at least one seam showing the difference.

The complete absence of contractions across all dialogue — including Stephen's 'I will eat with you in the small parlour if you wish' — makes every line of speech sound written rather than spoken. Period register is a real craft concern, but King's historical intuition (and his stated rule) is that dialogue must *sound* like breath first. One or two contractions in informal exchanges would not break the period; their total absence breaks the illusion of voice.
suggestions: Break up the junior-officer speech with one moment of re-ordering or self-correction — Augustine starting a sentence and redirecting it. 'Your men may — Stephen will direct their placement. After the bishop is inside, come to me in the lay parlour.' One seam makes the whole exchange feel spoken.

Redistribute the council-schedule exposition. Augustine doesn't need to recite all three days in sequence. Let him name one agenda item aloud, let Stephen confirm, let the narration compress the rest: 'He named the three mornings and the assignments, and Stephen wrote them as he spoke.' The reader gets the information; the dialogue stays in the room.

Give Possidius one line in the three-week recovery that is distinct in rhythm from Augustine's speech — a half-finished thought, a repetition, something that marks a man still operating with a head wound. The injury is tracked medically but not in voice. A man three weeks out from a wound to the head does not speak in complete, balanced sentences.

Find one moment for Stephen to speak a full sentence unprompted that is not advice to Augustine about eating — something that reveals his interior by accident, that he half-catches and stops. The chapter tells us Stephen is 'managing in the way Stephen had managed across the years'; give the reader one glimpse of what's beneath the management through dialogue, not narration. King would say: show us the crack, not just the wall.

sections.assessment: This chapter demonstrates sophisticated structural intelligence across a four-week compression that very few writers pull off cleanly. The architecture is genuinely impressive: a slow, weighted arrival; bureaucratic afternoon; a first-week summary; the growing Stephen observation; Possidius's departure; a coda. Each beat occupies exactly the space its emotional weight demands. The section breaks ('---') earn their placement — each one marks a genuine shift in time and register, not just a scene change but a change in what kind of attention the prose is asking of the reader. The refrain 'He went to compline' and 'The disc was cold' function the way King's best repeated images function: they accumulate meaning rather than decorating it. Short sentences land like strikes — 'Augustine watched the cart come.' alone on a line; 'The first week passed.' as a three-word paragraph; 'Augustine was at the gate with Stephen.' as pivot after a massive block — these are exactly how King controls pace: density, then release. The departure scene is the structural peak and it holds. The chapter's primary pacing problem is the opening paragraph, which is catastrophically dense. King's rule is that the first line, then the first paragraph, exist to pull the reader through the door. Here the door is a loading dock. The actual hook — Augustine, alone at a gate, waiting — is buried under fifty-two words of logistical context before we even reach the cart. Additionally, some interior reflection passages in the third-week section run past their natural stopping point, explaining what the prose has already shown. The writing earns its compression everywhere except where it forgets to earn its expansion.
good_examples: 'Augustine was at the gate with Stephen.' — Six words after a sprawling 120-word paragraph. The gear-shift is perfect. King uses this technique constantly: exhaust the reader with the world's weight, then land them on solid ground with a short declarative. The sentence lands like a boot on stone.

'Augustine watched the cart come.' — A full line, alone, doing nothing but holding space. This is white space as punctuation. The reader's eye stops. The cart keeps coming. The tension is in the pause, not the action.

'The first week passed.' — Three words as a paragraph. This is structural confidence. King would say this is a writer who trusts the reader. The summary that follows earns its length because this sentence has already done the emotional compression.

'The disc was cold.' — As a repeated refrain across the chapter's three uses, this does exactly what King's best recurring images do in Pet Sematary or The Shining: it accumulates. By the final use, 'The disc was cold' carries all the previous coldness with it. The reader feels the weight without being told to.

'*Possidius. / *Aurelius.*' — The italicized exchange reduced to names only is a masterpiece of pacing. After thirteen hours of formal historical prose, two men greet each other with only their names. The stripped dialogue is faster than any description of the moment could be.

'He went to compline.' — As a section-ending sentence, repeated across the chapter, this is structurally elegant. It locates Augustine in his life. It closes each section without summarizing it. King ends chapters this way — with a character moving toward the next thing, not away from what just happened.
bad_examples: The entire opening paragraph — 'The cart came to the basilica gate at the second hour of the afternoon, having travelled the lower coast road through the night and the morning under armed escort that the proconsul's office at Carthage had grudgingly provided after the senior bishop had pressed for three days. The escort was four mounted men in the colours of the Numidian garrison and a junior officer who had ridden ahead to the gate at the noon hour to give Augustine the time to prepare.' — This is the first thing the reader encounters. It is logistical inventory. The hook is absent. King's opening paragraphs almost never do this — they find the human thing first, then give you the world. Augustine waiting at the gate is the hook. It is not in the first paragraph. It is in the third.

'The cart carried Possidius. Possidius was lying on a pallet of straw under a canvas roof, with two of his own clergy from Calama at his head and feet, and with a freedman of the Calama diocese walking at the off-side of the mule who had been a stretcher-bearer in the army in his earlier life and had been hired by the deacon at Calama to keep the pallet steady on the road.' — The anaphoric repetition of 'Possidius' is right. But the freedman's biography is not. It is the wrong detail in the wrong place. We don't need to know the freedman's history here. King would cut from 'canvas roof' to 'two clergy at his head and feet' and stop. The freedman detail belongs later if anywhere.

'He thought: this is the household I have made. He thought it in the surprised way a man at forty-nine thinks a thing he has not, until that moment of looking at the people in the room with him, taken the trouble to think.' — This is the chapter explaining its own emotional moment. The sentence before — 'Augustine looked, on the morning of the third day of the third week, at the three of them at the desk' — has already done the work. King's rule: if you've shown it, you've shown it. Explaining that Augustine is surprised to think the thought he is thinking is the prose catching its own wheel.
suggestions: Restructure the opening. Begin with 'Augustine was at the gate with Stephen.' Then give us the wait, the watching, the road. Then bring the cart. Move the logistical backstory (proconsul's escort, three days of pressing) into the summary passage after the cart arrives — it fits better there as context than as an opening.

Cut the freedman's biography from the arrival paragraph. 'a freedman of the Calama diocese who had been a stretcher-bearer in the army in his earlier life and had been hired by the deacon at Calama to keep the pallet steady' — this is three clauses of information that stops the cart fifty yards from the gate. Free him. Let him just be the man at the off-side of the mule.

In the third-week writing-room scene, cut the second and third 'He thought' sentences after 'this is the household I have made.' Trust the image. Augustine looking up from the parchment at his household is complete. The analysis of how households are made is the writer explaining the scene to himself.

The Stephen-observing-Possidius sequence across days four, five, and six is the chapter's best structural move — but consider whether it needs the word 'recognised' repeated on day six. 'Augustine recognised that the looking had a meaning and that the meaning was a meaning he was not, on the present accounting of his attention, prepared to take in' — this is close to over-working a thing the reader has already understood. One fewer sentence here sharpens the cut.

The departure scene's final exchange — Stephen at the desk, Augustine not saying the thing he walked back intending to say — is structurally near-perfect. It could end on 'He got up. He went out of the writing room.' The chapter-house reflection that follows ('the watching was the work I did in your house for thirty-three years') is slightly too tidy as meaning-making. Consider moving that moment to the November bronze-box scene, where it would land in the coda rather than as a pendant to the departure.

sections.assessment: This chapter operates on four simultaneous thematic registers — the unspeakable carried in silence, the accidental household as family, the watchfulness that is love, and the object that has not yet found its man — and none of them announce themselves. They emerge wholly from the situation and the characters: a bishop in 403 CE receiving a wounded friend, a deacon with feelings the protocols of his household forbid him to name, and a bronze disc that has been cold for fourteen years. The most impressive achievement is the treatment of Stephen's unspoken attachment to Possidius. The author never names it, never editorializes, never lets Augustine name it either. The theme lives entirely in observation — in the looking that 'went on for the count of three and then Stephen would turn his face to the wall.' The protocols of religious life become the architecture of repression, and the repression becomes the tragedy, and the reader feels it without being told to feel it. The bronze disc is equally well-managed: it functions simultaneously as plot object (the thriller's MacGuffin), as spiritual barometer (warm once at Ostia, cold ever since), and as thematic statement about carrying a burden whose intended recipient has not yet appeared. Augustine is the steward, not the destination — a deeply melancholy idea that the chapter embeds in pure action: he takes the box down, checks, puts it back, goes to compline. The 'watching' motif — Monica's thirty-three years, Augustine's sixteen, Stephen's unnamed vigil — threads all three registers together at the end. Where the chapter loses its footing is in the rare moments where Augustine's internal voice states the theme rather than enacting it, briefly collapsing the subtext into text.
good_examples: 'Stephen looked at it the way a man looked at a face he was not, by the protocols of the household, permitted to look at the way he wished to look at it; and the looking went on for the count of three and then Stephen would turn his face to the wall.' — The entire forbidden feeling rendered in administrative language. The church's protocols are the cage. Never named, never editorializerd. This is how subtext works.

'He had been the desk the household had organised itself around.' — The accidental-family theme in one sentence, emerging entirely from Augustine's surprised self-recognition. It earns its place because it arrives after eight years of accumulated evidence the reader has just witnessed.

'He was managing in the way Stephen had managed across the years he had been in the household, which was the way of a man who had decided some long time ago that the management was the work and that the management did not require permission to feel less than it appeared to feel.' — The repression is characterological, not imposed. Augustine has been watching this for nine years. The theme of feeling that cannot surface is in the observation, not in commentary.

'He had been judging men, in the back of his attention, for sixteen years. He had not, in those sixteen years, judged any of them worthy.' — The disc's coldness as loneliness. The guardian outlasting the task. Entirely subterranean.
bad_examples: 'He thought: I will, in the years to come, watch better.' — This states the lesson the chapter has already delivered through Monica's echo. King's rule: the theme has already done its work by this point; spelling it out as an internal resolution diminishes it. The reader has understood. Trust them.

'In the autumn the Donatist proceedings in the next valley to Calama concluded with the conviction of two priests and the quiet exile of a third.' — The closing Donatist passage functions as plot housekeeping but does no thematic work. The real ending is Augustine in the chapter house alone in the light from the small high window. Everything after the gate scene is diminuendo where the chapter needed one clean note and silence.
suggestions: Cut or compress the final Donatist paragraph. The thematic closure is already complete when Augustine sets the cold disc back and goes to compline — that's the chapter's last word, and the rhythm earns it. The legal outcome of the attack belongs in the next chapter's opening, not tacked to this one's ending.

Excise 'He thought: I will, in the years to come, watch better.' Monica's words have already delivered this. Let the chapter close on the action — the box returned, the shelf, the going to compline — and allow the reader to arrive at the watching-theme themselves. The subtext is doing exactly what it should; don't tip it into text at the finish line.

The moment Augustine recognizes what Stephen's looking means and 'was not, on the present accounting of his attention, prepared to take in' is excellent — but consider whether 'recognised that the looking had a meaning and that the meaning was a meaning he was not... prepared to take in' slightly over-explains the recognition itself. Augustine standing from the bedside stool and going out is sufficient. The reason can remain entirely in behavior.

The bronze disc's warmth at Ostia — mentioned twice — is the chapter's most resonant symbol: the one moment it was warm was the moment of his mother's death. This implies the disc responds to something Augustine has not yet understood about the transfer. That implication is currently buried. One additional beat — Augustine not-quite-articulating why it was warm that night and not since — would deepen the object's mystery without naming it.

sections.assessment: This chapter earns its period in the most demanding way possible: through institutions, not costume. The 403 CE setting isn't window-dressing — it's structural. The Donatist attack on Possidius of Calama is a real historical event. Eraclius is the historical man who will succeed Augustine. Monnica died at Ostia in 387. Adeodatus died at Tagaste around 390. The Council of Carthage in 403 is the actual council. The proconsul's office, the Numidian garrison, the episcopal hierarchy, the canonical hours, the freedman's legal status — all of it is precise and load-bearing. Characters are products of their era at the bone-level: their world is organized by canonical hours, imperial administration, church politics, and the specific violence of the Donatist schism. The author avoids every anachronism of thought and speech. This is the discipline King means when he talks about knowing your world so completely it stops being research and becomes air. The single significant failure is sensory. The chapter is set in the second week of August in North Africa — one of the hottest places on earth in summer — and the word 'heat' never appears. The harbor of Hippo Regius is gestured at five times ('harbour road,' 'harbour quarter,' 'harbour livery') but never smelled, never heard, never felt underfoot. The wine Augustine drinks is taken 'for the warmth' — in August, in Algeria. The material culture of late Roman North Africa (the specific weight of North African ceramics, the quality of linen, the smell of a mule on a coastal road in summer) is thin everywhere except the bronze box. The institutional world is A-level work. The sensory world is C-level. King would tell you: the Donatist proceedings are perfect; now make me feel the heat coming off the courtyard stones.
good_examples: 'the junior officer who had ridden ahead to the gate at the noon hour to give Augustine the time to prepare' — the military escort's cautious, protocol-bound behavior is grounded in the specific violence of the Donatist period; a Roman officer in 403 CE North Africa would absolutely be nervous about this assignment

'the Donatist proceedings in the next valley to Calama concluded with the conviction of two priests and the quiet exile of a third. The attack on the bishop on the road in August was the principal evidence.' — this is the real historical event (the Donatist attack on Possidius of Calama, c. 403 CE) rendered with the specificity of a court record; it earns the whole chapter's period

'the wound on the shoulder remained the swelling it had been on arrival but did not progress to the deeper rot the diocese at Calama had feared' — pre-antibiotic medicine accurately rendered; 'deeper rot' is period-appropriate terror, not modern clinical detachment

'the night his mother died at Ostia in 387, the night his son died at Tagaste in 389' — real dates, real places, real biographical facts woven into the supernatural object's history; this is cultural grounding through biography, not description
bad_examples: 'He drank the wine for the warmth and not for the taste.' — the scene is set in the second week of August at a North African coastal city. A man drinking wine 'for the warmth' in Hippo Regius in August is an anachronism of sensation. The author has forgotten what month it is.

'He ate the bread without paying attention to it.' — bread is accurate; inattention is fine; but this is also the only food described in thirty pages of a story set in a specific diocese of late Roman North Africa. The material culture of the table — the ceramic bowl, the quality of the oil, the specific weight of the bread — is a missed opportunity to anchor us in the physical world of 403 CE

The harbor of Hippo Regius is referenced five times ('harbour road,' 'harbour quarter,' 'harbour livery,' 'harbour road' again, the post-coach) but never evoked through sense. Hippo Regius was a working port on the Algerian coast. There should be smell — fish, salt, tar, animal dung on the road up from the docks. The geography is named but not inhabited.
suggestions: Give August its due. The second week of August in Hippo Regius (modern Annaba, Algeria) is brutally hot. The heat should register somewhere — in the quality of light on the courtyard stones, in the way the canvas roof of the cart traps it, in Augustine standing at the gate for two hours and feeling it. One sentence of heat does more cultural grounding than a paragraph of institutional detail.

The harbor of Hippo Regius is doing no work. On the morning Possidius's cart comes up the 'harbour road,' give us one specific detail of what that road smells and sounds like. A port town in 403 CE North Africa has a specific sensory signature. Use it once, precisely, and you've placed us there.

The bronze disc is described as cold three times. Cold relative to what ambient temperature? In August in North Africa, 'cold' in the hand is a more violent and specific sensation than it would be in a temperate climate — lean into it. The coldness of the disc against the expected warmth of August is a detail that earns both the season and the object's strangeness.

The Donatist schism is the correct engine for this thriller, but its street-level violence could register once in the body. The circumcelliones — the Donatist shock troops who carried clubs because scripture forbade swords — are the specific reason a bishop traveling in 403 CE North Africa needed an armed escort. Name the weapon. That detail costs one word and buys the whole historical conflict.

sections.assessment: This prose has a real engine under it — the liturgical repetition, the terse declarative sentences after long coiling ones, the deliberate withholding of emotional labeling. These are intelligent choices that earn their keep. But the chapter has a consistent overwriting problem in its most important moments: the emotional subtext. King's rule is that the prose should be invisible precisely when the stakes are highest, and this author does the opposite — when something hurts, the prose explains what it means. 'The recognition was the recognition' is the clearest symptom. The author also has a structural tic — the 'on [his/the] reading of [X]' construction — that appears so frequently it becomes visible as style rather than transparent storytelling. The 2nd draft = 1st draft minus 10% principle would clean most of this. Opening paragraph is a 118-word single sentence that could be three. Stephen's interiority is over-explained at the exact moment restraint would gut-punch the reader. The disc sequences work. The farewell scene nearly works, then over-explains itself on the last beat. Grade slips from A territory to B because the overwriting clusters around the chapter's emotional core — which is the one place King would never let the prose get in the way.
good_examples: 'Augustine watched the cart come.' — Single line after long wind-up. King's tempo. Does everything.
'The disc was cold.' — Tolls like a bell throughout. Repetition with purpose. This is the chapter's best prose device.
'He went to compline.' — Invisible close. No commentary, no feeling-statement. The reader does the work.
'Augustine put his hand on Possidius's hand at the side of the pallet. The hand was cool.' — The shift from 'Possidius's hand' to 'The hand' is excellent. Quiet and precise. King would approve.
'The four letters took the afternoon.' — Six words doing an afternoon's work. Exactly right.
'Yes, father.' / Augustine did not, at the door, mention the address.' — The old address slipping out, then the non-response. Showing, not telling. The chapter's best beat.
bad_examples: 'He had recognised it. The recognition was the recognition. The recognition would, on his understanding of his own household and his own friends and his own habits of carrying what required carrying, be the kind of thing he carried without naming...' — The author explaining the chapter's emotional core to a reader who already feels it. 'The recognition was the recognition' is a writer noticing their own device. Cut the entire paragraph after 'He had recognised it.' Show Augustine picking up the pen. Done.

'He was managing in the way Stephen had managed across the years he had been in the household, which was the way of a man who had decided some long time ago that the management was the work and that the management did not require permission to feel less than it appeared to feel.' — 'management / manage / management / management / manage / management' — six forms of the same word in one sentence. The thought is good but it's been hammered flat.

'He thought it in the surprised way a man at forty-nine thinks a thing he has not, until that moment of looking at the people in the room with him, taken the trouble to think.' — Telling the reader how to receive the moment. Cut to 'He thought it with something close to surprise.' Or better: just cut the gloss entirely. 'He thought: this is the household I have made. He went on with the letter.' Trust it.

'The cart came to the basilica gate at the second hour of the afternoon, having travelled the lower coast road through the night and the morning under armed escort that the proconsul's office at Carthage had grudgingly provided after the senior bishop had pressed for three days.' — 118-word single sentence as the chapter's first. King opens with a hook, not a briefing. 'The cart came through the gate at the second hour. Four Numidian soldiers rode with it.' Then earn the backstory.
suggestions: Find every instance of 'on [his/the/Augustine's] reading/understanding/accounting/present trajectory/honest count' — there are at least a dozen. These period-flavored hedges are a house style tic. Cut half of them cold. The ones that remain will read as intentional.

Apply the 10% rule specifically to every paragraph where the prose explains emotional subtext. The Stephen-watching-Possidius-sleep sequence, the 'recognition was the recognition' paragraph, and the closing chapter-house reflection are all 30-40% too long. Let the reader sit in the moment.
The opening sentence needs to be broken into three. Information density in the first paragraph is front-loaded to the point of exhaustion — by the time the cart arrives the reader is already tired.

'The face will go back to being the face it was' is used three times. First use: resonant. Third use: the author leaning on a line that worked. Change the formulation on the third use or cut it entirely.

The farewell scene between Augustine and Possidius at the gate is the chapter's best scene and it almost sticks the landing. Cut the final gloss: 'the look acknowledged the recognition without naming it; and the not-naming was the not-naming both of them, by the protocols of the household and the friendship and the office, were going to do.' The look breaks and they embrace. That's the sentence. Let it end there.

overall_assessment: King-style analysis complete. Overall grade: B+. Strengths: Backstory & Characterization, Pacing & Structure, Themes & Subtext, Cultural Specificity & Time Period.
